---
title: "Conway's Blobs"
jupyter: brainvomit
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

Inspired by Conway's *Game of Life*, I wanted to extend the experiment to simulate not only the rules but also the game-theoretic consequences of what those rules imply, given a population of agents that can sense and interact with the universe and each other. 

BlobSim is something between a cellular automaton and simple ecological sandbox. It is a small, toroidal gridded world. Each cell can hold one **blob** — a simple creature with energy and metabolism. Time advances in discrete steps. At each step, every blob inspects its surroundings, selects an action (move, eat, reproduce, or rest) based on a swappable 'intelligence engine', and the universe runs the energy accounting/conflict resolution via asserted laws. Run out of energy and you die; eat well and you reproduce. The grid itself generates food at a configurable rate. 

You can imagine how BlobSim arises from the Game of Life via abstraction of just two key ideas:

1. Agent state is defined not as a binary dead/alive, but as a continuous energy level.
2. Conway's behavioral rules are a function of the local neighborhood; BlobSim introduces probabilistic decision-making based on the underlying swappable 'engine'.

ie BlobSim is reducible to Conway's Game of Life if the engine is deterministic and the energy level is binary.

<!-- TODO: what question this world is meant to explore. -->

## Design

The grid wraps around at the edges (a blob walking off the right side reappears on
the left). Every step runs the same fixed sequence:

1. observe: each blob gathers local information
2. decide: engines choose an action based on observations and internal state
3. conflict resolution: since time steps are discrete, the ordering of actions must be resolved 
4. energy accounting/execution: conservation laws are enforced, and the energy ledger is updated; resolved actions are applied to the grid and the blobs
5. grid update: the environment adds energy to the grid according to its rules

The behaviour of a blob (the "engine"), the behaviour of the environment (decaying vs.
replenishing), and how conflicts are resolved are each swappable without
touching the rest.

<!-- TODO: more on the sensing model and the action costs, in your words. -->

<!-- TODO: the personal hook — why this was worth building. -->

## Universal laws

A set of invariants about this world are asserted, to maintain a somewhat coherent and internally consistent universe:

- **Conservation of energy.** At any moment, the energy held by living blobs, plus the
  energy left in the grid, plus everything spent so far, equals what the world
  started with (plus anything the environment has since added)
  - **Reproduction and predation only moves energy.** A new blob is funded entirely out of its
  parent's energy; a predator's kill transfers energy from the victim to the attacker
  - **Death is permanent.** A blob whose energy falls to the death threshold is
  removed forever
- **Determinism.** The same starting seed reproduces the exact same run
- **One blob per cell.** No two blobs ever occupy the same square.

## Example

A finite grid with no replenishment can only end one way — the blobs eat through the
available energy and die out:

![Two kinds of blob draw down a finite grid until none are left.](/assets/images/blobsim/sc1_decay_extinction.gif)

One run's population and total energy over time:

```{python}
#| label: setup
import pandas as pd
import plotly.io as pio
import plotly.graph_objects as go
pio.templates.default = "plotly"

import blobsim
```

```{python}
#| label: live-sim
from blobsim import (
    Simulation, SimulationConfig, SpeciesConfig, BlobAttributes,
    RegeneratingEnvironment, ActionType, SensingLevel, MOORE,
)
from blobsim.species import GrazerRules

attrs = BlobAttributes.create(
    species="grazer", max_energy=10.0, offspring_energy=3.0,
    reproduction_threshold=7.0, base_metabolic_cost=0.1, move_cost=0.2,
    reproduce_cost=0.5, observation_radius=1, sensing_level=SensingLevel.BASIC,
    allowed_actions=frozenset(ActionType), allowed_directions=MOORE,
)
config = SimulationConfig(
    grid_size=30,
    environment=RegeneratingEnvironment(rate=0.05, capacity=2.0, initial_cell_energy=1.0),
    species=[SpeciesConfig(rules_factory=GrazerRules, attributes=attrs, count=25, initial_energy=5.0)],
    seed=42,
)
sim = Simulation(config)
traj = pd.DataFrame([
    {"step": s.step, "population": s.n_alive, "total_energy": s.e_blobs + s.e_grid}
    for s in sim.iterate(steps=200)
])

fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_scatter(x=traj.step, y=traj.population, name="population", yaxis="y1")
fig.add_scatter(x=traj.step, y=traj.total_energy, name="total energy (blobs + grid)", yaxis="y2")
fig.update_layout(
    height=300, margin=dict(l=0, r=0, t=10, b=0), xaxis_title="step",
    yaxis=dict(title="population"),
    yaxis2=dict(title="energy", overlaying="y", side="right", showgrid=False),
    legend=dict(orientation="h", y=1.12),
)
fig
```

The run opens with a boom — the grazers find food and grow ~10x in ~20 steps, overshooting the grid's energy capacity. At that inflection point, the population draws energy down faster than it regenerates, so the energy level drops and the population crashes. The two mechanisms settle into a gradually dampening feedback loop that behaves as out-of-phase swings, converging toward the sustainable stready-state population.

<!-- TODO: add your own read if you want to emphasize something else. -->

## Benchmark runs/base cases

For purpose of sanity checking, each base case below computes a target *analytically from the
rules* (a conservation identity, an expected lifespan, a stable population) and then
checks the simulation derives it within tolerance. 

### Predicted lifespan

**The rule.** A lone blob lives in two phases: an *active* phase where it can still
afford to move (paying a higher average cost per step) and a *resting* phase, below
that affordability threshold, where it can only idle at the fixed metabolic cost until
it starves. Adding the expected length of both phases gives a closed form,

$$E[\tau] \;=\; \frac{E_0 - B}{E[c]} \;+\; \frac{B}{M} \;+\; (\text{boundary correction}),$$

with starting energy $E_0$, resting cost $M$, average active cost $E[c]$, and the
affordability threshold $B$ below which moving is no longer possible. For the test
parameters this predicts **≈ 186 steps**. Across 50 independent runs the observed
lifespans cluster tightly around the prediction (mean ≈ 185):

![Lifespan over 50 lone-blob runs. The distribution sits right on the closed-form prediction (red), with observed mean (black dashed) ≈ 185.](/assets/images/blobsim/bench_lifespan.png)

<!-- TODO: why the two-phase split, in your words. -->

### Guaranteed extinction on a finite grid

**The rule.** If the environment adds no energy, then every step at least some energy
is spent and never returned, so the world's total can only fall. With a finite
starting amount $E_0$ and a smallest per-step metabolic cost $m$, every blob must die
within

$$T_\text{max} = \left\lceil E_0 / m \right\rceil$$

steps — and reproduction can't postpone it, because offspring are funded from parents
(energy only moves, it isn't created). And it isn't a one-off: across many seeds every
finite-grid run climbs, then collapses to zero without exception.

![Twelve independent finite-grid runs — every one reaches zero. Extinction isn't a quirk of one seed; it's guaranteed by the energy budget.](/assets/images/blobsim/bench_extinction.png)

<!-- TODO. -->

### Propagation, ceiling, then death

**The rule.** Give blobs a rich but *non-replenishing* grid and they multiply fast — but
this is not a *cycle*, it's a one-way trip: **propagation → ceiling → death**. The
population spreads until it hits a ceiling set by *space*, not energy (with one blob per
cell it can't exceed the number of cells); then, with no new energy coming in, the
crowd draws the finite store down and starves all at once. There is no recovery — the
energy to fund a second wave was already spent.

![A population spreads to fill the grid, hits the one-per-cell ceiling, then starves to extinction.](/assets/images/blobsim/sc4_boom_bust.gif)

<!-- TODO: contrast with the *regenerating* example up top, which DOES oscillate because
energy is replenished — the difference is the environment, not the blobs. -->

### Carrying capacity

**The rule.** When the grid replenishes, the population settles where energy coming in
balances energy spent. Equating the two gives a predicted stable size

$$N^* \approx \frac{\text{rate}\times(\text{cells})}{\text{rate} + \text{avg cost per blob}} \approx 106.$$

Starting from very crowded and very sparse, both runs converge to roughly the same
level — evidence it's a genuine attractor, not an artifact of where you start.

::: {layout-ncol=2}
![Sparse start — grows toward the steady level.](/assets/images/blobsim/sc5_sparse.gif)

![Crowded start — relaxes down to the same level.](/assets/images/blobsim/sc5_dense.gif)
:::

Across many runs of each kind, both starting densities collapse onto the *same* band
within ~30 steps — sparse runs rising into it, dense runs falling into it — which is the
signature of a genuine attractor. (It settles a little *above* the simple $N^*$ line — an
expected overshoot: the formula assumes the blobs are perfectly mixed, but real ones
spread out, so fewer of their moves collide and get wasted and a few more survive.)

![Smoothed population over many runs. Sparse starts (blue, from 10) rise and dense starts (orange, from 300) fall onto one common band — the same attractor regardless of where you start. Dashed line is the mean-field prediction N* ≈ 106.](/assets/images/blobsim/bench_carrying.png)

<!-- TODO: the surprise — observed settles ~25% above the prediction, because the
mean-field math assumes blobs are well-mixed; real blobs cluster and spread out, so
fewer of their moves collide and get wasted. -->

### A lone blob's energy

**The rule.** A single blob on a fast-replenishing grid absorbs more each step than it
spends, so its energy climbs quickly to the maximum it can hold and then stays pinned
there — steady, with essentially no variation. Several runs, same story every time:

![Energy of a lone blob across several runs — each shoots to the maximum it can hold (20) within a few steps and stays pinned.](/assets/images/blobsim/bench_lone_energy.png)

<!-- TODO. -->

### Extinction threshold

**The rule.** Whether a population survives hinges on how fast the grid replenishes.
There is a **critical rate**: below it, each blob can't absorb enough to replace what
it spends, so on average it leaves fewer than one surviving offspring and the line
dies out; above it, survival becomes possible. The crossover is sharp — a phase
transition.

::: {layout-ncol=2}
![Below the critical rate — the population dies out.](/assets/images/blobsim/c2_extinction.gif){width="320"}

![Above the critical rate — the population survives.](/assets/images/blobsim/c2_survival.gif){width="320"}
:::

Sweeping the rate and measuring how often the population survives (10 seeds per rate)
draws out the transition: survival jumps from near-zero to near-certain across a narrow
band around the critical rate ≈ 0.075.

![Survival probability versus grid replenishment rate. The sharp rise across ~0.075 is the phase transition.](/assets/images/blobsim/bench_threshold.png)

<!-- TODO. -->

### Grazers vs. walkers

**The rule.** Put two strategies on one grid: undirected **random walkers** and
**grazers** that move toward the richest neighbouring cell. With identical
metabolism, the grazers absorb more per step, so they reproduce faster and eventually
take over. Greater efficiency compounds into dominance.

![Energy-seeking grazers outcompete random walkers.](/assets/images/blobsim/c3_competition.gif)

<!-- TODO: what decides the margin / how fast displacement happens. -->

<!-- TODO: which complex scenarios you actually want to explore next. -->

<!-- TODO: ordered takeaways, e.g.
1. Conservation is the backbone — every base case is ultimately energy bookkeeping.
2. On a finite grid, extinction is guaranteed; reproduction only moves energy around.
3. Crowding (one-per-cell), not total energy, caps the population at its peak.
4. Survival has a sharp threshold in the replenishment rate.
5. The simplest population prediction runs ~25% low because it over-counts collisions.
-->

## Emergent behavior

My primary interest was in discovering strong emergence (after having verified weak emergence). Coexistence niches, non-obvious optimization strategies, and other non-predictable patterns that arise from the interaction of simple rules. 

### Predation

To introduce direct inter-species interactions, a blob can now **attack** an adjacent blob of a species it preys on. Hunt success is determined by energy: the attacker wins only if it currently holds *more* energy than its victim; a kill transfers the victim's energy to the attacker; a failed hunt costs the attempt. Crucially, predators stay **omnivores**: every blob still
grazes the grid as before; hunting is purely opportunistic.

<!-- TODO: why energy-as-strength is the right combat rule, in your words. -->

### The ouroboros

I discovered the conditions to produce an ouroborous: A eating B in one place while B eats A in another, the
dividing line forever circling. 

The missing ingredient is a **cycle of three**: A eats B, B eats C, C eats A — rock,
paper, scissors. Now no one is strongest, and from a random scatter the grid
spontaneously sorts itself into travelling patches that chase each other forever:

![Three omnivores in a cycle (A eats B, B eats C, C eats A) self-organise into rotating domains — each colour endlessly eating into the next and being eaten from behind.](/assets/images/blobsim/cyclic_ouroboros.gif){width="320"}

![The cyclic world at five steps — domains advance and retreat as each colour eats into the one it dominates.](/assets/images/blobsim/cyclic_frames.png){width="320"}

The population never converges: each population oscillates, peaking after the species
it eats, all three locked in the same rotation. Two views of that rotation — the
populations over time (left), and the same whole-grid A:B:C mix drawn step by step as a
path on a triangle (right), where each corner is "all one species", the centre an even
split, and colour runs dark (start) → bright (end):

::: {layout-ncol=2}
![Populations over the run — sustained, out-of-phase oscillation; each peak chases the prey it eats.](/assets/images/blobsim/cyclic_populations.png)

![The whole-grid mix drawn as a path, orbiting the three-way centre — never reaching a corner or settling.](/assets/images/blobsim/cyclic_orbit.gif){width="240"}
:::

The system never diverges into corners (no species ever wins) and never reaches the centre (convergence) — its population mix is in perpetual orbit. This universe cycles ~ 0.5 million units of energy through births, kills, and metabolism over 1.5k steps, and the conservation ledger still balances to machine precision.

<!-- TODO: your own read — why the 3-cycle rotates and the 2-cycle can't (chirality / no
cyclic dominance), and whatever you find most striking watching the gif. -->

### The ouroboros is fragile to size

The cyclic coexistence isn't guaranteed — it **depends on the world being big enough**.
Coexistence is a *spatial* effect: the three only persist because the rotating patches
keep them locally separated. Shrink the grid and random fluctuations let one species
vanish — and then the cycle breaks catastrophically. Say C dies out; now A has lost its
only predator (C ate A), so A grows unchecked and drives B extinct too. Three becomes
one. The tail-eating snake eats *itself*.

Running the same setup at different grid sizes (5 seeds each), and asking how often all
three survive to step 600, gives a sharp transition around ~32 cells a side:

![Fraction of runs keeping all three species, versus grid size. Below ~30 the cycle collapses to a monoculture; above ~45 all three reliably coexist.](/assets/images/blobsim/cyclic_finite_size.png)

The two regimes look completely different on the grid — a small world is eaten down to a
single colour, while a large one sustains the rotation indefinitely:

::: {layout-ncol=2}
![Small world (22×22) — diversity collapses to one survivor.](/assets/images/blobsim/cyclic_collapse.gif){width="320"}

![Large world (60×60) — all three coexist, chasing forever.](/assets/images/blobsim/cyclic_ouroboros.gif){width="320"}
:::

This is the grid version of a known result in cyclic competition (Reichenbach *et al.*,
*Nature* 2007): all three survive only when the world is bigger than a single rotating
patch — one spiral's width. Below that, chance picks a winner and diversity is lost.

<!-- TODO: your own read on why bigger = more robust (independent patches buffering
fluctuations), if you want to expand it. -->
